question_text: Design a ride-sharing system like Uber.
model_answer_summary: Strong answers identify real-time location at scale as the core challenge, use a geo-index for O(1) nearby driver lookup, and articulate the consistency trade-off explicitly.
common_mistakes: Naive SQL lat/lng query instead of a geo-index
Polling every second without discussing WebSocket trade-offs
Not addressing delayed location updates

model_answer_summary: Strong answers compare fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read trade-offs explicitly, handle celebrity accounts as a special case, and address cache invalidation on follow/unfollow.
common_mistakes: Applying fan-out-on-write uniformly without addressing celebrity accounts
No discussion of cache invalidation strategy
Ignoring the difference between home timeline and user timeline

question_text: Design a URL shortener like bit.ly.
model_answer_summary: Strong answers pick a collision-resistant encoding scheme, discuss 301 vs 302 redirect implications, and reason about read-heavy scaling with a CDN or cache layer.
common_mistakes: Not discussing collision resolution strategy
Ignoring the 301 vs 302 redirect distinction and its caching impact
Underestimating read-to-write ratio (100:1 is typical)

model_answer_summary: Strong answers compare token bucket vs sliding window algorithms, discuss the race condition in distributed counters, and propose a Redis-based atomic solution with Lua scripts or INCR+EXPIRE.
common_mistakes: Using a fixed window counter without acknowledging the burst problem at window edges
No discussion of the distributed synchronization problem
Ignoring the consistency vs availability trade-off under network partition

question_text: Design a notification system (push, email, SMS).
model_answer_summary: Strong answers model notification as a pipeline (event → queue → dispatcher → channel adapters), discuss at-least-once vs exactly-once delivery guarantees, and handle deduplication at the consumer.
common_mistakes: Calling third-party providers (APNs, FCM) synchronously in the request path
No discussion of retry logic and idempotency
Treating all channels (push, email, SMS) identically without acknowledging their different latency and reliability profiles

question_text: Design a distributed cache like Redis.
model_answer_summary: Strong answers explain consistent hashing for node distribution, compare LRU/LFU eviction policies with a concrete use case, and address the thundering herd problem on cache miss.
common_mistakes: Not explaining how nodes are assigned keys (consistent hashing)
Ignoring cache invalidation — only discussing cache population
No discussion of hot keys or the thundering herd problem

model_answer_summary: Strong answers compare trie vs inverted index for prefix matching, discuss pre-computation of top-k suggestions, and address how to handle ranking updates without blocking reads.
common_mistakes: Proposing a naive DB LIKE query without acknowledging the latency problem
No discussion of how to rank or personalize suggestions
Not addressing stale suggestions when trending topics change rapidly

model_answer_summary: Strong answers center the design on idempotency (retries must not double-charge), use a double-entry ledger for auditability, and distinguish between payment intent creation and capture as separate operations.
common_mistakes: No idempotency key strategy — retries cause double charges
Mutable balance field instead of an append-only ledger
Not distinguishing authorization from capture in the payment flow
